_comment: Authoring input for the fixture pipeline (WP-0). Each entry pins a scenario to a date and area of operations so tools/fixtures/index.mjs can freeze real geodata into src/scenarios/fixtures/<id>/. Coordinates are the scenario startPosition. Observed weather is applied to realistic INCIDENT scenarios only — NOT to demo_basic / demo_sar, the onboarding tutorials, whose real-day weather can close launch bays (>30kt gust) and must stay launchable.
_dateProvenance: Two kinds of realDate, and the distinction is deliberate. `dateKind: "documented"` means the scenario models a specific real incident and the date is that incident's real date — the weather that was actually over that place that day. `dateKind: "representative"` means the scenario models a mission TYPE rather than one named event; the date is a real, seasonally-appropriate day at the real location, and the weather is genuinely observed, but it is not tied to a specific historical callout. Never present a representative date as a documented one.

scenarios.name: FEMA USAR — Hurricane Ian, Fort Myers Beach
scenarios.note: Hurricane Ian landfall near Fort Myers Beach / Estero Island. Real ERA5 winds (59kt sustained, 110kt gust) correctly ground the fleet — the sim refusing to launch into documented hurricane conditions IS the realism.

scenarios.name: CAL FIRE / USFS — Dixie Fire, Northern Flank
scenarios.note: Dixie Fire, 2021 — the 4 August run on the northern flank that overran Greenville. Real fire-weather for the day the scenario models.

scenarios.name: SAR — Coastal / Ocean Beach
scenarios.note: Representative San Francisco summer marine-layer morning.

scenarios.name: USCG District 1 — Atlantic Mariner SAR
scenarios.note: Representative late-autumn Nantucket Sound day (25kt gust, 49°F) — the season that actually drives Atlantic mariner SAR callouts, and hard enough to matter without closing the launch bays. 2024-11-12 was tried first and came back at 42kt gust, which grounds the fleet; Fort Myers/Ian is the one scenario that should teach that lesson.

scenarios.name: CBP Big Bend — Desert Humanitarian SAR
scenarios.note: Representative Chihuahuan Desert midsummer day — heat is the operative hazard in Big Bend humanitarian SAR.

scenarios.name: LAPD SkyWatch — Skid Row Welfare Grid
scenarios.note: Representative Los Angeles winter night — cold-weather welfare checks are the real driver for this mission type.

scenarios.name: NYPD Aviation — Times Square MCI
scenarios.note: Representative Manhattan late-November day — a high-density crowd event in the AO the scenario models.

scenarios.name: LAPD SIS — Hollywood Bowl Response
scenarios.note: Representative Los Angeles summer evening — Hollywood Bowl performance season.

scenarios.name: DHS CIKR — Port of LA Chemical Response
scenarios.note: Representative San Pedro Bay early-autumn day. Wind direction governs plume drift, which is the operative decision here.

scenarios.name: ATF Group IX — Oakland Stash Surveillance
scenarios.note: Representative East Bay autumn day — the clear, low-wind spell that favours long static surveillance.

scenarios.name: USSS — Presidential Visit SF Advance Sweep
scenarios.note: Representative downtown San Francisco early-summer day.

scenarios.name: Multi-Agency — SF to Albany Hills Suspect Pursuit
scenarios.note: Representative East Bay late-spring day across the pursuit corridor.

scenarios.name: FBI HRT — Compound Siege (ISR/Entry/Extract)
scenarios.note: Representative Mojave high-desert spring day — afternoon wind is the limiting factor on a long ISR hold.

scenarios.name: CBP Eagle Pass — Rio Grande Relay
scenarios.note: Representative south-Texas early-spring day on the Rio Grande.

scenarios.name: CBP Laredo — Rio Grande 25-Mile Relay Patrol
scenarios.note: Representative Laredo-sector spring day across the 25-mile relay corridor.
